subject: ali-yahya

sectors_excited.name: AI x crypto convergence
sectors_excited.why: This is the thesis I care about most right now. AI is concentrating power in a handful of closed labs and flooding the internet with synthetic content; crypto is the natural counterweight — decentralized ownership of models and data, verifiable provenance, open and incentive-aligned networks rather than corporate monopolies. The two technologies need each other: AI gives crypto genuinely useful work to coordinate, crypto gives AI decentralization, attribution, and aligned ownership. Generational, not a quarter-long narrative.

sectors_excited.name: DePIN / decentralized compute
sectors_excited.why: Token incentives can bootstrap real-world physical infrastructure — compute, GPUs, storage, wireless, energy — faster and at lower capex than centralized incumbents. AI's insatiable demand for compute makes decentralized GPU and compute markets especially compelling this year: a real bid for real hardware, not just speculation. This is where crypto's coordination machine meets a tangible bottleneck.

sectors_excited.name: Proof of personhood / decentralized identity
sectors_excited.why: Once AI makes bots indistinguishable from people, a cryptographic, privacy-preserving way to prove unique humanity becomes foundational internet infrastructure. This is one of the clearest cases where crypto solves a problem AI itself creates.

sectors_excited.name: Stablecoins and onchain payments
sectors_excited.why: The clearest product-market fit in all of crypto. Dollars on open, global, instant, near-free rails — already moving real volume, already useful to people who don't care about crypto. The closest thing we have to a proven killer app.

sectors_excited.name: Consumer apps / decentralized social
sectors_excited.why: Infrastructure has finally matured — cheap L2 blockspace, account abstraction, better wallets — so it's time to build apps people actually want, not just more infra. User-owned social graphs and open consumer protocols are where the next wave of real usage comes from.

sectors_concerned.name: Low-float / high-FDV token launches
sectors_concerned.why: The prevailing launch structure — tiny circulating float against enormous fully-diluted valuations — concentrates upside in insiders and hands the public structurally bad entries. It's corrosive to trust and to the long-term health of the market, and as an industry we should want better.

sectors_concerned.name: Speculation outrunning utility
sectors_concerned.why: A large share of onchain attention right now is pure casino with no durable value accrual. Memecoins can be a legitimate proof of distribution and a cheap onboarding funnel, but if speculation is the main thing being built rather than a side effect of building, that's a problem.

sectors_concerned.name: US regulatory hostility
sectors_concerned.why: Enforcement-by-litigation and the absence of clear, fit-for-purpose rules push builders and liquidity offshore and chill legitimate innovation. It's the single largest avoidable headwind facing the sector.

tokens_excited.name: ETH
tokens_excited.why: Conviction is at the ecosystem level: the leading decentralized settlement and smart-contract platform, and the home of most of the primitives I care about. Spot ETF approval (19b-4s cleared in May, launch still pending) is a structural adoption tailwind, not a trade call.

tokens_excited.name: SOL
tokens_excited.why: Ecosystem-level view, not a price target: the high-performance chain currently capturing the most consumer and retail activity, and a credible venue for the cheap-blockspace consumer apps I'm excited about.

risk_regime.why: I deploy across cycles on a multi-year horizon, and the structural setup is the strongest it's been: Bitcoin and (pending-launch) Ethereum ETFs opening institutional rails, the April halving behind us, and a genuine technology wave in AI x crypto. This is conviction to keep building and deploying through the cycle, not a near-term timing call — near-term price action will be choppy and I'm not predicting it.

notes: I'm a long-horizon VC, not a token trader — my real conviction lives at the sector and primitive level (AI x crypto, DePIN/compute, identity, stablecoins, consumer apps), much of which is still early or pre-token. All items here are marked extrapolated: they reflect my standing thesis framing rather than a freshly recorded, datable quote, and I won't fabricate citations. tokens_concerned is intentionally empty — I hold no specific token shorts; my structural worry is the low-float/high-FDV launch dynamic, captured under sectors_concerned.
